source.title: Dorsal anterior cingulate cortex and the value of control.

confidence_rationale: This 2016 Nature Neuroscience update defends the EVC theory of dACC against foraging-based alternatives and stresses that the region's activity tracks control allocation across many experimental contexts without needing task-mode-specific subregions. That cross-context generality is the relevant structural analogue for the E2 mode question: a single representational resource can serve multiple task functions when a controller specifies the read-out weights. Supports MECH-E2-DUAL-FUNCTION framed as single-process-plus-gated-readout, and weakens any framing that requires architecturally distinct E2 populations per mode. Confidence 0.55: the evidence is precedent-by-analogy rather than direct forward-model evidence.
failure_signatures: If dACC itself turns out to decompose into task-mode-specific subregions (foraging value, cognitive effort, conflict), the single-process-plus-readout precedent collapses and the default expectation for E2 shifts back toward a dual-substrate account.
If the brain's mode arbitration is local to sensory-motor forward models (cerebellum, M1) rather than a downstream controller, the Shenhav-style controller analogy may not transfer to E2.

mapping.source_claim_statement: dACC is best understood through the EVC theory as specifying the optimal allocation of control by evaluating expected value of control -- a single integrative function that accounts for the region's sensitivity to reward, conflict, effort, and foraging-like decisions, without requiring mode-specific sub-divisions.
mapping.ree_translation: Translated to REE, the finding suggests that a single forward-model substrate (E2) can subserve multiple task modes (attribution vs. evaluation) without requiring architecturally distinct sub-populations, provided that a controller signal (dACC-analogue) gates read-out. This directly supports the single-process-plus-readout formulation of MECH-E2-DUAL-FUNCTION.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper's domain is cognitive control allocation, not forward-model prediction. Its authority over the E2 mode question is indirect. A second caveat is that dACC may be coupled to forward-model regions (cerebellum, ventral striatum) rather than sitting inside them; thus the controller-and-substrate decomposition may not be interchangeable with the E2-internal mode question.
mapping.source_context: Nature Neuroscience Perspective (2016), update to the 2013 EVC theory. Explicitly contrasts with foraging-based accounts of dACC, arguing the EVC framework is more comprehensive. Relevant here because it sharpens the single-controller claim against decompositional alternatives.

confidence_components.notes: Nature Neuroscience Perspective, canonical follow-up to Shenhav 2013. Mapping fidelity is moderate-low for the same reason as the 2013 entry: the subject is control allocation, not forward-model reuse. Transfer risk is non-trivial because dACC's architecture may not be a good template for E2's.
